Copy Results
Results copied to clipboard
About
Version
Version %1$s
About App
AnyCheck is a comprehensive Android root detection tool that identifies Magisk, KernelSU, APatch and other root management frameworks, as well as Xposed/LSPosed hook frameworks.
Detection Methods
Magisk Detection
17 checks
KernelSU Detection
8 checks
APatch & Generic Root
5 checks
Xposed / LSPosed
13 checks
Advanced Root Checks
19 checks
Luna Methods
22 checks
System Integrity
Multiple checks
Risk Levels
Note: When only MEDIUM or lower risk items are detected, the summary shows \u201cRoot May Possibly Exist\u201d instead of \u201cRoot Framework Detected\u201d.
Author & Repository
GitHub
xingguangcuican6666
Repository
xingguangcuican6666/AnyCheck
Our Philosophy

Unlike certain tools that simply shout \u201cDetected!\u201d without providing any evidence, context, or explanation, AnyCheck is built on a different principle: if something is found, you deserve to know exactly what it is, why it matters, and how to fix it. The habit of issuing an authoritative verdict with zero supporting detail is nothing more than fearmongering dressed up as security. AnyCheck may not yet cover every possible detection scenario, but every result it delivers comes with clear reasoning and actionable guidance\u2014so you can actually understand what was found and do something about it.
Initializing checks\u2026
Magisk checks complete
KernelSU checks complete
Generic checks complete

No action required.
KernelSU Files Detected
KernelSU Files
KernelSU-specific files or directories found.
No KernelSU-specific files detected.
The following KernelSU files were found: %1$s. KernelSU is a kernel-based root solution. ksud is the KernelSU daemon and /data/adb/ksu is its working directory.
No known KernelSU file paths were found on this device.
To remove KernelSU: Use KernelSU Manager → Uninstall, or flash a non-KernelSU kernel/stock boot image via fastboot.
KernelSU Manager Detected
KernelSU Manager Packages
KernelSU Manager or related apps installed.
No KernelSU Manager packages found.
Found KernelSU packages: %1$s. The KernelSU Manager app is the management interface for KernelSU. Its presence confirms KernelSU is installed.
No known KernelSU package names were installed.
Use KernelSU Manager to uninstall, then flash a stock kernel.
KernelSU in Kernel Version
KernelSU Kernel Version
KernelSU signature found in kernel version string.
No KernelSU signature in kernel version string.
Could not read kernel version.
The kernel version string contains KernelSU markers: %1$s. KernelSU patches the kernel and embeds its version in the kernel string. Kernel: %2$s
The kernel version string does not contain KernelSU markers.
Failed to read /proc/version: %1$s
Flash a stock (non-KernelSU) kernel via fastboot or recovery.
Ensure /proc/version is accessible.
KernelSU Database Found
KernelSU Database
KernelSU SQLite database detected.
KernelSU database not found.
KernelSU uses a SQLite database to store allowed package UIDs and policy rules. Found at: %1$s.
No KernelSU database was found at known locations.
The database is removed when KernelSU is fully uninstalled.
KernelSU Mount Points Detected
KernelSU Mount Points
KernelSU-related mount points found.
No KernelSU mount points found.
Could not read mount information.
KernelSU uses OverlayFS to overlay module files. Keywords found in /proc/mounts: %1$s.
No KernelSU-specific mount entries found.
Failed to read /proc/mounts: %1$s
Mount points are managed by KernelSU. Removed upon uninstallation.
Ensure /proc/mounts is accessible.
KernelSU Processes Running
KernelSU Processes
KernelSU daemon processes are running.
No KernelSU daemon processes found.
Could not enumerate processes.
Found processes: %1$s. ksud is the KernelSU userspace daemon that handles su requests.
No KernelSU process names found in running process list.
Process enumeration failed: %1$s
Processes stop after KernelSU is removed and device rebooted.
Ensure /proc is accessible.
KernelSU Kernel Interface
SELinux Permissive Mode
KernelSU kernel interface detected.
No KernelSU kernel interface found.
SELinux is in permissive mode (potential KernelSU indicator).
KernelSU exposes a kernel interface at /sys/kernel/ksu or /proc/sys/kernel/ksu. This confirms KernelSU is compiled into the running kernel. Found: %1$s.
No KernelSU-specific kernel interfaces were detected.
SELinux enforce status is 0 (permissive). KernelSU and other root solutions often set SELinux to permissive mode to allow unrestricted root access. This significantly reduces device security.
Flash a stock kernel without KernelSU to remove this interface.
Set SELinux to enforcing mode: `setenforce 1`. This setting persists only until reboot unless KernelSU/Magisk policies override it.
KernelSU System Properties
KernelSU-specific system properties found.
No KernelSU system properties found.
The following KernelSU properties were found: %1$s. These properties are set by KernelSU and confirm its presence.
No KernelSU-specific system properties were detected.
These properties are removed when KernelSU is uninstalled.
KernelSU Loop Device Detected
KernelSU Loop Device Check
A loop device mounted at a KernelSU path was found, indicating KSU LKM mode is active.
No KernelSU loop device mounts found.

Found loop device(s) mounted at KSU paths: %1$s. KernelSU LKM (Loadable Kernel Module) mode mounts its filesystem image via a /dev/loop* device at /data/adb paths. This is a definitive indicator that KernelSU is active in LKM mode.
No /dev/loop* devices were found mounted at KernelSU-specific paths (/data/adb/ksu, /data/adb/modules). KSU loop device is not active.
Update KernelSU manager and re-patch the boot image. Use SUSFS or ZygiskNext to hide the loop device mount.
APatch Detected
APatch
APatch root framework detected.
APatch not detected.
APatch is a kernel-based root solution similar to KernelSU. Evidence found: %1$s. APatch patches the kernel boot image to enable root access.
No APatch files or packages were found.
Use APatch Manager to uninstall, then flash a stock boot image.
SU Binary Detected
SU Binary
su binary found at system locations.
No su binary found at common system paths.
The su (superuser) binary was found at: %1$s. The su binary allows apps to request elevated root privileges. Its presence in system paths indicates the device has been rooted.
The su binary was not found at standard system paths.
Remove the su binary by booting into recovery and deleting it, or restore via stock system image flash.
Root Management Apps Found
Root Management Apps
Root management or exploit apps detected.
No root management apps found.
Found: %1$s. These apps provide root access management, exploit capabilities, or root-related functionality that indicates a rooted device.
No known root management or exploit app packages were found.
Uninstall these apps via Settings → Apps or using adb uninstall.
Test-Keys Build Detected
Debug Build Detected
Build Signature
Device is running a test-keys signed build.
Device is running a debug build.
Build appears to use official release keys.
Build tags: \'%1$s\', Fingerprint: \'%2$s\'. Test-keys builds are signed with unofficial keys, indicating a custom or rooted ROM. Official Google-signed builds use \'release-keys\'.
Build tags: \'%1$s\'. Debug builds have additional debugging capabilities enabled and may have relaxed security restrictions.
Build tags \'%1$s\' indicate official release key signing.
Flash an official OEM ROM signed with release-keys via fastboot or OTA.
Use a release/production build for better security.
Busybox Found
Busybox
Busybox binary detected.
No Busybox binary found.
Busybox was found at: %1$s. Busybox provides Unix utilities typically not found on Android. While not root itself, it is commonly installed alongside root frameworks.
Busybox was not found at common installation paths.
Busybox is removed when the associated root framework is uninstalled.
Dangerous / Piracy Apps Detected
Dangerous / Piracy Apps
Potentially dangerous, piracy, or game-cheat apps found.
No dangerous or piracy apps detected.

Found %1$d app(s): %2$s. These packages include black-market stores, in-app purchase bypass tools, game cheats, and spyware-like apps. Their presence indicates system compromise or intentional security policy violations.
None of the known dangerous/piracy/cheat packages were found.
Uninstall these apps immediately via Settings → Apps or adb uninstall.
Extended Root-Cloaking Apps Detected
Extended Root-Cloaking Apps
Additional root-hiding or code-injection framework apps detected.
No additional root-cloaking apps detected.
Found: %1$s. Cydia Substrate is a runtime code injection framework frequently used to hide root. HideMyRoot and HideRoot variants conceal root indicators from apps.
Cydia Substrate and HideMyRoot/HideRoot variants were not found.
Uninstall root-cloaking apps via Settings → Apps.
SU Binary via PATH Enumeration Detected
SU Binary via PATH Enumeration
su binary found via dynamic PATH enumeration.
No su binary found via PATH enumeration.

su binary found at: %1$s. This check dynamically reads the PATH environment variable (like rootbeerFresh) in addition to well-known static paths, catching su binaries placed in non-standard directories that are still on the executable search path.
su was not found in any static or PATH-derived directory.
Remove the su binary and restore a stock system image.
System Paths Mounted Read-Write
System Paths Mount Check
Critical system paths mounted rw: %1$s.
No critical system paths mounted read-write.
Could not run mount command.

The following paths that should be read-only are mounted read-write: %1$s. This is detected by parsing the \`mount\` command output with SDK-version-aware field offsets (rootbeerFresh technique). A rw-mounted /system indicates the system partition has been modified.
mount command output shows all sensitive paths are read-only.
The mount command returned no output.
Remount system partition as read-only: \`adb shell mount -o remount,ro /system\`.
Ensure /proc is accessible.
AVB Boot State
AVB Boot State: YELLOW
AVB Boot State: ORANGE (Unlocked)
AVB Boot State: RED (Integrity Failure)
AVB Boot State: Unknown
Verified Boot state is GREEN.
Verified Boot state is YELLOW (custom ROM / self-signed).
Verified Boot state is ORANGE -- bootloader is UNLOCKED.
Verified Boot state is RED -- boot image verification FAILED.
Unexpected Verified Boot state: %1$s.
AVB state property not available.
Could not determine AVB boot state.
ro.boot.verifiedbootstate=%1$s. The device booted from verified, unmodified system partitions. This is the strongest boot integrity assurance.

ro.boot.verifiedbootstate=%1$s. The bootloader verified the boot image against a user-installed key rather than a manufacturer key. This indicates a custom ROM or user-enrolled AVB key. The device may be running unofficial firmware.
ro.boot.verifiedbootstate=%1$s. The bootloader is unlocked and no signature is enforced. The device can boot arbitrary unsigned images. This is a strong indicator of a rooted or modified device.
ro.boot.verifiedbootstate=%1$s. The device failed to verify the integrity of the boot image. This may indicate a modified bootloader or corrupted system partition.
ro.boot.verifiedbootstate=%1$s is not a standard value. Expected: green, yellow, orange, or red.
ro.boot.verifiedbootstate is empty or inaccessible. This is normal on older devices (pre-Android 8) that do not support AVB 2.0.
Exception reading AVB properties: %1$s
Flash official OEM firmware to restore GREEN verified boot state.
Re-lock the bootloader via \'fastboot flashing lock\' after restoring stock firmware.
Restore factory firmware via fastboot or OTA.
Check device firmware integrity.
TEE Key Generation: Software Only
TEE Key Generation
AES key was generated in software -- no hardware TEE backing.
AES key was generated inside secure hardware (%1$s).
TEE key generation check could not be completed.

A probe AES key was generated via AndroidKeyStore but KeyInfo reports it was created in the software emulation layer. This strongly suggests the device is an emulator, or the TEE has been bypassed (e.g. by a Keymaster/KeyMint shim module). Sensitive cryptographic material is NOT protected by secure hardware.

A probe AES key was generated via AndroidKeyStore and KeyInfo confirms it resides in %1$s. This check cannot be spoofed by Magisk property hiding because it exercises the actual TEE subsystem via the Binder-based Keymaster/KeyMint HAL.
Exception during probe key generation: %1$s
Use a real device with a hardware TEE for secure key operations.
Hardware-Backed RSA Key: Software Only
Hardware-Backed RSA Key
RSA key pair is in software keystore, not secure hardware.
RSA-2048 key pair resides in secure hardware (TEE/StrongBox).
Keystore attestation check could not be completed.

The AndroidKeyStore RSA key was generated in the software emulation layer rather than in a hardware TEE. This may indicate an emulator, a rooted device with Magisk hiding active, or a device that does not have a hardware-backed keystore.
RSA-2048 key generated via AndroidKeyStore resides inside the Trusted Execution Environment or StrongBox HSM. Private key material never leaves secure hardware.
Exception during RSA key generation/inspection: %1$s
Use a device with hardware-backed key storage for sensitive operations.
Root Daemon Processes Running
Root Daemon Processes
Active root framework daemon(s) detected in process list.
No known root framework daemons found in process list.
Could not scan process list.

The following root-related processes are running: %1$s. lspd is the LSPosed/Xposed framework daemon. ksud is the KernelSU daemon. magiskd is the Magisk daemon. zygiskd is the Zygisk companion daemon. These confirm an active root framework even if file artifacts are hidden.
Scanned /proc for lspd, ksud, magiskd, zygiskd, riru, apd, and other known root framework daemons. None were found.
Exception reading /proc: %1$s
Uninstall the root framework and reboot to stop these daemons.
System Property Tampering
System Property Tampering
System properties show signs of modification.
No system property tampering detected.

The following property inconsistencies were detected: %1$s. Magisk resetprop can modify any system property after boot, but Android framework constants baked into the app JVM at launch cannot be changed retroactively -- a mismatch proves tampering.
Build.TAGS vs live ro.build.tags, security patch cross-check, fingerprint vs device name, and Magisk prop checks all passed. System properties are consistent with the stock build.
Restore stock boot image to reset system properties to factory values.
Su Binary (Extended Scan)
Su Binary (Extended Scan)
Su binary or root management files found.
No su binary or root management files found.
The following su-related paths were found: %1$s. Presence of a su binary is a strong indicator that the device is rooted.
Scanned %1$d known su binary paths and checked PATH. No su binary was found accessible on this device.
Remove all root frameworks (Magisk, KernelSU, APatch, SuperSU) and restore the stock boot image.
Magisk Files Detected
Magisk Files
Magisk-specific files or directories found on the filesystem.
No Magisk-specific files detected.
The following Magisk-specific files were found: %1$s. These files are created by Magisk during installation and runtime. Their presence strongly indicates that Magisk is installed on this device.
None of the known Magisk file paths were accessible or present.
To remove Magisk: Open Magisk Manager → Uninstall → Complete Uninstall. Or flash the stock boot.img via fastboot: `fastboot flash boot stock_boot.img`.
Magisk Directories Detected
Magisk Directories
Magisk working directories found.
No Magisk working directories found.
Directories %1$s exist. Magisk uses /data/adb/magisk as its primary working directory and /data/adb/modules to store installed modules. These are created during Magisk installation.
Magisk working directories were not found.
Uninstall Magisk via Magisk Manager or reflash stock boot image.
Magisk Database Found
Magisk Database
Magisk SQLite database detected.
Magisk database not found.
The Magisk database (magisk.db) stores policy rules, module configurations, and superuser grant records. Found at: %1$s.
No Magisk SQLite database was found at known locations.
The database will be removed when Magisk is fully uninstalled.
Magisk App Detected
Magisk App Packages
Magisk Manager or related apps installed.
No Magisk Manager packages found.
The following Magisk-related packages were found: %1$s. These apps are the primary management interface for Magisk. Their presence confirms Magisk installation.
No known Magisk Manager package names were found installed.
Uninstall via Magisk Manager first (to properly remove root), then uninstall the Magisk Manager app itself.
Magisk SU Binary Found
Magisk SU Binary
Magisk su or core binaries detected.
No Magisk su or core binaries found.
Magisk installs its su binary and core components to manage root access. Found: %1$s. These binaries handle root permission requests and policy enforcement.
Magisk core binaries were not found at known paths.
These binaries are removed when Magisk is uninstalled via Magisk Manager → Uninstall.
Magisk Unix Socket Detected
Magisk Unix Socket
Magisk daemon socket found in /proc/net/unix.
No Magisk daemon socket found.
Magisk runs a background daemon that listens on a Unix socket. The socket name \'%1$s\' was found in /proc/net/unix, indicating the Magisk daemon is currently running.
No Magisk-related socket names were found in /proc/net/unix.
Could not read /proc/net/unix: %1$s
The socket disappears when Magisk is uninstalled and the device is rebooted.
Magisk Mount Points Detected
Magisk Mount Points
Magisk-related mount points found in /proc/mounts.
No Magisk mount points found.
Magisk uses bind mounts to overlay modified files onto the system without actually modifying the underlying partition. Keywords found: %1$s.
No Magisk-specific mount entries found in /proc/mounts.
Could not read /proc/mounts: %1$s
Mount points are managed by Magisk daemon. They are removed upon uninstallation.
Suspicious System Props
Magisk System Properties
Suspicious or Magisk-related system properties found.
No suspicious Magisk-related system properties found.
The following properties suggest root access or Magisk presence: %1$s. ro.debuggable=1 and ro.secure=0 are typical on rooted/debuggable builds. ro.magisk.version is set by Magisk directly.
System properties appear to be in their stock configuration.
These properties are set by Magisk or during device rooting. Restore the stock boot image to reset these properties.
Zygisk Library Detected
Zygisk Library
Zygisk (Magisk in Zygote) library files found.
No Zygisk library files found.

Zygisk is Magisk\'s feature that runs in the Zygote process, enabling code injection into every app. Found: %1$s. This allows bypassing many detection methods and enables powerful module functionality.
Zygisk library files were not found. Either Zygisk is disabled or Magisk is not installed.
Disable Zygisk in Magisk settings or fully uninstall Magisk.
MagiskHide / Modified Fingerprint
MagiskHide / Fingerprint
MagiskHide artifacts or modified build fingerprint detected.
No MagiskHide artifacts or fingerprint modifications detected.
Evidence: %1$s. MagiskHide (or its successor DenyList) attempts to hide Magisk from specific apps. A modified build fingerprint may indicate SafetyNet bypass attempts.
No MagiskHide files found and build fingerprint appears unmodified.
Disable MagiskHide/DenyList in Magisk settings. Use a stock, unmodified build fingerprint. Consider using a Play Integrity fix module carefully.
Magisk Processes Running
Magisk Processes
Magisk-related processes are currently running.
No Magisk processes found running.
No Magisk processes found.
The following Magisk processes were found running: %1$s. magiskd is the Magisk daemon that manages root requests. These processes confirm Magisk is active on the device.
No Magisk-related process names were found in /proc.
Process enumeration could not be completed: %1$s
Processes will stop after Magisk is uninstalled and device rebooted.
Hidden Magisk Manager Detected
Hidden Magisk Manager
An app disguised as Magisk Manager was found.
No hidden Magisk Manager detected.
Could not scan for hidden Magisk Manager.
Magisk Manager can be hidden under a random package name to evade detection. Found potential hidden manager: %1$s.
No apps with Magisk Manager characteristics were found under non-standard package names.
Package scan could not be completed: %1$s
Open the app and uninstall Magisk, or use adb to remove the package.
Magisk /dev Files Detected
Magisk /dev Files
Magisk-specific files or sockets found in /dev.
No Magisk /dev files or sockets found.
Found: %1$s. Magisk uses the /dev filesystem (tmpfs) to create control sockets and mirror points that it can access from any SELinux context. These files only exist on devices with active Magisk.
No Magisk-specific files were found under /dev.
Uninstall Magisk to remove these /dev artifacts.
Zygisk Library Injected
Zygisk Library
Zygisk native library is loaded in this process.
No Zygisk library found in process memory maps.
No Zygisk library found in process memory.

Zygisk library found in /proc/self/maps: %1$s. Zygisk (Zygote + Magisk) injects its native library into every app process by hooking the Zygote process before forks. This confirms Zygisk is actively running.
No Zygisk native library was found in /proc/self/maps.
Could not read /proc/self/maps: %1$s
Disable Zygisk in Magisk settings or uninstall Magisk.
Magisk Stub App Detected
Magisk Stub App
App with Magisk stub APK characteristics found.
No Magisk stub app detected.
Package(s) with Magisk stub metadata: %1$s. Magisk\'s hide feature repackages itself as an innocent-looking app. The stub retains Magisk\'s content provider authority and specific permission set.
No installed packages match the Magisk stub APK signature.
Open the stub app and uninstall Magisk through it.
Magisk SELinux Context Detected
Magisk SELinux Context
Magisk-related SELinux context found.
No Magisk SELinux contexts detected.
No Magisk SELinux context found.

Found: %1$s. Magisk injects custom SELinux policy rules and some versions run under their own SELinux context (u:r:magisk:s0). A non-standard SELinux context for a root-related process confirms Magisk activity.
No Magisk-related SELinux contexts found in /proc/*/attr/current.
Could not read /proc/self/attr/current: %1$s
Remove Magisk to restore standard SELinux policy.
Magisk APEX Overlay Detected
Magisk APEX Overlay
Magisk overlay inside /apex detected.
No Magisk APEX overlay detected.

Found: %1$s. Magisk (v24+) can inject modules into APEX (Android Pony EXpress) partition libraries by bind-mounting over them. The \'orig\' directory is created as a backup of the original APEX content. These artefacts are only present when Magisk modules are active.
No Magisk-specific files were found inside /apex.
Disable APEX-targeting modules in Magisk Manager or uninstall Magisk.
Magisk File Descriptors Detected
Magisk File Descriptors
This process has open file descriptors pointing to Magisk paths.
No Magisk-related file descriptors found in /proc/self/fd.
Found in /proc/self/fd: %1$s. Magisk keeps open FDs to its mirror mount points and socket files. The presence of these FDs in an app process confirms Magisk has touched this process.
No open file descriptors point to Magisk paths.
Remove Magisk to clear these file descriptor references.
Native Bridge Injection Detected
Native Bridge
Riru/Zygisk native bridge injection detected.
No suspicious native bridge injection found.

ro.dalvik.vm.native.bridge = \'%1$s\'. Riru (a predecessor of Zygisk) hijacks the ART native bridge mechanism by replacing this property with its own loader library. A suspicious value here is a strong indicator of Riru/Zygisk-based injection.
ro.dalvik.vm.native.bridge is \'%1$s\' \u2014 stock value.
Remove the Riru or Zygisk Magisk module to restore the normal native bridge.
Zygisk Companion Daemon Detected
Zygisk/Magisk Daemon Processes
Zygisk/Magisk daemon processes are running.
No Zygisk/Magisk daemon processes found.

Found daemon process(es): %1$s. Zygisk Next (a standalone Zygisk implementation) runs privileged daemon processes named zn-daemon, zn-nsdaemon, and zn-zygisk-companion. Magisk itself runs as \'magiskd\'. These are definitive indicators of active root injection.
No Zygisk Next or Magisk daemon process names were found in /proc/*/cmdline.
Uninstall Magisk or the Zygisk Next module to remove these daemon processes.
Magisk Hook Latency Anomaly
File Access Timing
Suspicious file access latency suggests Magisk DenyList hooks.
No suspicious timing anomaly for Magisk file access.
Timing check inconclusive.

File.exists(\'%1$s\') took %2$sms (%3$s\u00D7 slower than baseline). Magisk\'s DenyList patches intercept filesystem calls to hide Magisk files. This interception adds measurable latency compared to accessing non-hooked paths.
File.exists() latency is within normal range (ratio=%1$s\u00D7, %2$sms). No Magisk DenyList hook latency detected.
Could not complete timing check: %1$s
Disable Magisk DenyList or uninstall Magisk.
Root/Hook Libraries in Process Memory
Root/Hook Libraries
Root or hook framework native libraries are loaded in this process.
No root/hook framework native libraries found in process memory.

Found in /proc/self/maps: %1$s. These are native libraries belonging to Magisk (libsu), Riru, and inline hook engines (Dobby, SandHook, Pine, Whale) used by Xposed-compatible frameworks. Their presence confirms active injection into this process.
No known root/hook library names were found in /proc/self/maps.
Remove the relevant root framework or module to prevent library injection.
Zygisk Detected via SELinux Exec Chain
Zygote SELinux Exec-Chain Label
The SELinux label inherited from zygote\'s exec chain indicates Magisk/Zygisk modified the zygote startup.
The SELinux exec-chain label from /proc/self/attr/prev looks normal.

Read /proc/self/attr/prev = \'%1$s\'. Normally zygote is exec\'d directly by init (u:r:init:s0), so any app process forked from zygote inherits prev=u:r:init:s0. With Magisk/Zygisk, a wrapper binary first runs under the zygote context (u:r:zygote:s0) and then exec\'s the real zygote, changing the inherited prev label. The observed label is anomalous and indicates Zygisk is present.
/proc/self/attr/prev = \'%1$s\'. The value is consistent with a clean zygote exec chain — zygote was started directly by init without a Magisk/Zygisk wrapper in between.
Could not read /proc/self/attr/prev: %1$s
Uninstall Magisk/Zygisk and reboot so zygote is exec\'d directly by init, restoring the normal SELinux exec-chain label.
Zygisk Hook Detected via smaps Dirty Pages
smaps Relocation-Section Dirty Pages
Private_Dirty pages were found in read-only relocation sections of system libraries, indicating Zygisk hook/unhook activity.
No suspicious Private_Dirty pages found in system library relocation sections.

Found r--p pages with Private_Dirty > 0 in: %1$s. Zygisk hooks system libraries (e.g. libandroid_runtime.so) by overwriting their GOT/relocation table (.data.rel.ro section). After the dynamic linker finishes, this section is remapped read-only (r--p). Zygisk\'s unhook in the forked app process writes original values back, creating private dirty pages in these otherwise clean r--p mappings — a definitive Zygisk fingerprint.
All r--p pages in libandroid_runtime.so, libart.so, libdvm.so, and libnativehelper.so have Private_Dirty = 0 kB. No Zygisk hook/unhook activity detected via smaps.
Could not parse /proc/self/smaps: %1$s
Disable Zygisk in Magisk settings and reboot, or uninstall Magisk entirely to prevent relocation table patching.
Magisk Environment Variables Detected
Magisk Environment Variable Remnants
Magisk-injected environment variables were found in /proc/self/environ.
No Magisk environment variable remnants found in /proc/self/environ.

Found in /proc/self/environ: %1$s. Magisk sets environment variables such as MAGISK_INJ_1, MAGISKTMP, and LD_PRELOAD during zygote injection. /data/app Package Directory Scan
An LSPosed, EdXposed, or Hide My Applist install directory was found directly in /data/app, bypassing PackageManager hooks.

OAT Deoptimisation Artefact Detected
Own OAT Integrity Check
Unexpected files or a corrupted OAT header found in this app\'s compiled-code directory, indicating hook-framework interference.
This app\'s OAT directory contains only expected artefacts and the OAT magic is valid.

LSPosed and similar hook frameworks deoptimise target-app methods by removing their compiled OAT machine code, forcing the ART interpreter to execute DEX bytecode instead (where hook trampolines fire). This leaves observable artefacts: extra DEX/ODEX files in the OAT directory, a corrupted OAT header magic, or a missing base.odex alongside an existing base.vdex. Anomalies found: %1$s.

This app\'s oat/ subdirectory contains only the standard artefacts (base.odex, base.vdex, optionally base.art) and the OAT file starts with the expected magic bytes (6f 61 74 0a), indicating no hook-framework deoptimisation.
Disable all LSPosed/Xposed modules that target this app, then reboot so ART recompiles the OAT file cleanly.

LSPlant or LSPosed native library (liblsplant.so / liblsposed.so) is mapped into this process.

LSPlant is the ART hooking engine that powers LSPosed. When LSPosed hooks a method in this app, it loads liblsplant.so into the process. This library appears as a named mapping in /proc/self/maps and cannot be hidden by Java-level hooks. Found: %1$s.

Delete the identified files/directories. Review any installed Magisk/KSU modules that may be creating these artefacts and remove them if no longer needed.
/debug_ramdisk Inconsistent Mount
/debug_ramdisk Mount Check
/debug_ramdisk is still mounted, which is abnormal on a production Android device.
/debug_ramdisk is not mounted. No anomaly.

/debug_ramdisk appears as an active mount in /proc/self/mounts. On stock Android this ramdisk path is unmounted after early init and should not be visible to apps. Its continued presence indicates either a non-standard boot image or that a root framework has left it mounted, potentially exposing kernel debug symbols or a writable root-namespace path.
/debug_ramdisk is not present in /proc/self/mounts. The device boot process looks normal.
Run \'umount /debug_ramdisk\' as root, or use a SUSFS/ZygiskNext configuration that unmounts it during boot. Re-patching with a clean stock boot.img will also resolve this.
MT Manager Artefact Detected
MT Manager Check
The MT Manager \'mt2\' folder was found, confirming the APK editor/file manager has been used on this device.
No MT Manager mt2 folder found.

Found MT Manager directory at: %1$s. MT Manager (a popular APK editor and file manager) creates an \'mt2\' folder in the root of internal storage by default. Its presence confirms MT Manager has been run on this device, indicating potential APK patching, module injection, or other file-level tampering.
No \'mt2\' directory found under /sdcard or /storage/emulated/0. MT Manager does not appear to have been used on this device.
Delete the /sdcard/mt2 directory. In MT Manager settings, change the MT2 path to a custom location or delete the directory after use.
Self-Compiled / Dirty Kernel Detected
Kernel Build Suffix Check
The kernel version string contains the \'-dirty\' suffix, indicating a locally compiled or uncommitted kernel build.
No \'-dirty\' suffix found in the kernel version string.

Kernel version: \'%1$s\'. The \'-dirty\' suffix is appended by the Linux build system when the source tree contains uncommitted changes at build time. This is a reliable indicator of a third-party, self-compiled, or custom kernel — commonly used alongside custom ROMs and root setups.
The kernel version string in /proc/version does not contain the \'-dirty\' suffix. The kernel appears to be a clean, official build.
Flash an official kernel or use SUSFS to spoof the kernel version string so it no longer contains the \'-dirty\' suffix.
dex2oat Anomaly Detected
dex2oat Check
A non-standard dex2oat configuration or LSPosed dex2oat wrapper was found, indicating a hook framework is active.
No dex2oat anomalies detected.

Detected: %1$s. LSPosed and some Xposed forks intercept the dex2oat compilation pipeline by placing a wrapper binary at /data/adb/lspd/dex2oat or by setting a non-standard dalvik.vm.dex2oat-filter property. These changes redirect DEX compilation through the hook framework, leaving detectable artefacts.
No non-standard dex2oat-filter property or LSPosed wrapper binary was found. dex2oat pipeline appears unmodified.
Update or reinstall LSPosed. If the issue persists, wipe the Dalvik cache (wipe /data/dalvik-cache) and reboot to force a clean dex2oat recompilation.
Scene Toolkit Detected
Scene Toolkit Check
The Scene performance toolkit app is installed or its local server port is open.
No Scene toolkit indicators found.

Scene detected via: %1$s. Scene (com.omarea.vtools) is a system-level performance and battery management tool that runs an accessibility service and optionally a local HTTP server on well-known ports (18080, 18081). Its presence indicates advanced system modification capability on this device.
Scene toolkit packages (com.omarea.vtools) were not found and no listening sockets on Scene\'s known ports (18080, 18081) were detected.
Disable Scene\'s accessibility service, close its local server from Scene settings, or hide Scene using HMA to prevent detection.
Hidden Process Groups Detected
Hidden Process Groups Check
Processes with a missing thread-group leader in /proc were found, indicating deliberate process hiding.
No hidden process group anomalies found.

Found processes whose thread-group leader (Tgid) is not visible in /proc: %1$s. Root daemon processes (LSPosed lspd, Magisk magiskd) sometimes hide their parent PID from the process table while leaving child threads visible. A Tgid that does not correspond to any /proc entry is a reliable indicator of a deliberately hidden process.
All visible /proc entries have thread-group leaders (Tgid) that are also visible in /proc. No hidden process groups detected.

Enable a software clone (分身) in system settings to create a new process group, which can mask the anomaly. Update root frameworks that may be causing the hidden group. Use ZN-AuditPatch or SUSFS for process hiding.
Netlink Socket Anomaly Detected
Netlink Socket Check
Non-standard Netlink protocol sockets were found in /proc/net/netlink, indicating possible kernel-level root activity.
No non-standard Netlink sockets found.

Found anomalies in /proc/net/netlink: %1$s. KernelSU and some Magisk variants use custom Netlink protocol families (outside the standard Android set of 0–22) to communicate between userspace and kernel modules. The presence of such sockets is a strong indicator of kernel-level root activity.
All Netlink sockets in /proc/net/netlink use standard Android protocol numbers (0–22) with normal socket counts. No anomalous kernel IPC detected.
Update KernelSU or Magisk to a version that hides its Netlink channels. There is currently no reliable user-space workaround for this detection method.
Root Process via Audit Log Detected
Audit Log Process Check
Root daemon SELinux contexts were found in kernel AVC audit log entries, revealing active root processes.
No root process indicators found in the kernel audit log.

Found in kernel AVC audit log: %1$s. Root daemons (magiskd, lspd, kpatchd) leave distinctive SELinux context strings (u:r:magisk:s0, u:r:su:s0, u:r:zygisk:s0) in AVC denial records even when they hide themselves from the standard process table. The native probe reads /dev/kmsg directly using raw syscalls that bypass Java-layer hooks.
No suspicious SELinux contexts or root process comm= values were found in /dev/kmsg or kernel logcat entries. The audit log appears clean.

Install the ZN-AuditPatch Magisk module to suppress root-related AVC log entries, or use SUSFS\'s audit log suppression feature. Alternatively, apply the Android security patch from September 2025 which addresses the audit log read vulnerability.

OEM Bootloader Unlock Enabled
The OEM unlock option is currently enabled in Developer Options (sys.oem_unlock_allowed=1).

sys.oem_unlock_allowed=1 indicates the OEM bootloader unlock toggle is active. While this alone does not mean the bootloader has already been unlocked, it is a prerequisite for bootloader unlocking and a prerequisite for many root frameworks. On some devices the property can only be set after the bootloader is already unlocked.
If you do not intend to unlock the bootloader, disable the OEM unlock toggle in Settings → Developer Options → OEM unlocking.

The Samsung Knox warranty counter has been tripped (ro.boot.warranty_bit=1), indicating that unofficial firmware or root has been installed.

ro.boot.warranty_bit=1. Samsung permanently sets this fuse-backed bit when an unofficial ROM, custom recovery, or root tool modifies the device. The Knox warranty is invalidated and Samsung Pay / Knox-dependent apps may refuse to work. This is a strong, hardware-backed indicator that root or custom firmware has been present.
The Knox warranty bit is hardware-backed and cannot be reset. If Knox-dependent features are required, the only option is device replacement or a full factory RMA with Samsung.

Seccomp Disabled (Kernel Anomaly)
Seccomp is disabled (value=0) in /proc/self/status – kernel does not enforce syscall filtering.

The Seccomp field in /proc/self/status is 0, which means the kernel does not support or has disabled the Secure Computing (Seccomp) mechanism. On Android 5.0+, all app processes should run in Seccomp filter mode (value=2). A value of 0 indicates a stripped or modified kernel that has removed Seccomp support — a common characteristic of older or insecure custom kernels shipped with some root solutions. This weakens process isolation and syscall filtering.

Do not use custom kernels that disable Seccomp. Reflash the stock kernel or a kernel that supports Seccomp filter mode. If you are using a root framework, check if your kernel build includes Seccomp support (CONFIG_SECCOMP_FILTER).
Seccomp Enabled
Seccomp is active (value=%1$d). Process syscall filtering is enforced.

Seccomp=%1$d in /proc/self/status. Value 2 (filter mode) is standard on Android 5.0+. Value 1 (strict mode) is uncommon but valid. Seccomp is functioning, meaning the kernel is enforcing per-process syscall filtering as expected.
Seccomp Field Missing
The Seccomp field was not found in /proc/self/status.

The Seccomp line was expected in /proc/self/status but was absent. This may indicate an old Linux kernel (pre-3.5) that predates Seccomp, or a modified kernel that strips the status field. Cannot confirm Seccomp is active.
Verify the kernel version supports Seccomp (Linux 3.5+). If running a custom kernel, check that CONFIG_SECCOMP and CONFIG_SECCOMP_FILTER are enabled.
Seccomp Status Check Error
Could not read /proc/self/status to check Seccomp status.
An exception occurred while reading /proc/self/status. This is unexpected as this file should always be readable by the current process.
Retry the detection. If the error persists, the device may have unusual /proc filesystem restrictions.

Remove Lucky Patcher, CorePatch, and any associated billing-bypass or signature-bypass packages. If system framework files have been patched, reflash the stock firmware. If CorePatch (an LSPosed module) is installed, disable or remove it. Review all installed apps for apps holding android.permission.INSTALL_PACKAGES that are not system packages.

Hidden Root Manager Detected (Launch Probe)
Root Manager with App Hiding Detected
Root Manager Launch Probe
A root manager package that is invisible to normal package queries was detected by successfully launching it via a system intent.
A root manager appears to be installed and actively hiding itself from package queries (app-hiding / DenyList is in use). The system jump to the root manager app confirmed its presence.
No hidden root managers detected via system launch probe.

Root manager detected via system launch probe: %1$s. An ACTION_MAIN + CATEGORY_LAUNCHER intent targeting the package was fired without specifying a particular activity class. If the system accepts the launch (no ActivityNotFoundException), the package is confirmed to be installed — even if conventional PackageManager queries (getPackageInfo, etc.) return NameNotFoundException. A successful launch that bypasses the PackageManager API is a strong indicator of app-hiding (Magisk DenyList / Shamiko / LSPosed hide).

System launch probes against all known root manager packages (Magisk, KernelSU, APatch, SuperSU, etc.) all threw ActivityNotFoundException, confirming no root manager is installed (even in a hidden state).

Disable app-hiding (Magisk DenyList / Shamiko / LSPosed hide) or uninstall the root manager. Then reboot and re-run AnyCheck. If the root manager is still required, remove it from the DenyList for this application.
Hide My Applist Active (Binder Probe)
Hide My Applist Binder Probe
Hide My Applist service detected via its hidden Binder interface injected into PackageManagerService.
No Hide My Applist service detected via PackageManager Binder probe.

HMA injects a hidden Binder service into the system PackageManager (android.content.pm.IPackageManager) using a custom transaction code (0x484D4144 — H-M-A-D). Sending this transaction with ACTION_GET_BINDER=1 returned a valid IHMAService Binder, confirming the HMA service is running inside system_server and actively filtering package queries for this app.
The HMAD transaction probe (0x484D4144) sent to the PackageManager Binder returned no Binder object, indicating HMA service is not active.
Disable Hide My Applist in LSPosed module manager or uninstall the HMA app, then reboot. If HMA must remain active, remove this application from its hide list.

Inline Hook Trampoline Detected (Native)
Native Inline Hook Detection
A hook trampoline was detected at the start of a core system function, indicating Frida, Dobby, ShadowHook, or a similar native hook framework is active.
No hook trampoline patterns detected in core libc/libdl function prologues.

Native inline-hook probe found: %1$s. Inline hooking replaces the first instruction(s) of a target function with a branch trampoline (e.g. ARM64 LDR Xn, #8; BR Xn or x86-64 JMP [RIP+0]) that redirects execution to attacker-controlled code. This is used by Frida gadget, Dobby, ShadowHook, and Pine to intercept native function calls without modifying shared libraries on disk.
The first bytes of open, read, write, mmap, dlopen, and dlsym match standard function-prologue patterns. No ARM64/ARM32/x86 hook trampolines detected.
Remove Frida gadget, Dobby, ShadowHook, Pine, or any other native inline-hook library injected into this process. Check for Zygisk/Riru modules that perform native function hooking.

LSPosed Parasite Mode Detected
LSPosed Parasite Mode Detection

LSPosed Parasite mode is active. In this mode lspd injects into the system_server process (the \'android\' system app, uid=1000) without Magisk or KernelSU. The android system process then posts an activation notification; tapping it opens the LSPosed Manager UI.
No LSPosed Parasite mode indicators detected (no injected libraries in system_server, no parasite config, no staging files).

The following LSPosed Parasite mode indicators were found: %1$s. LSPosed Parasite mode works by having a shell launcher (uid=2000) write lspd\'s code into system_server\'s memory via /proc/PID/mem. After injection, LSPosed hooks execute inside the android system process, which then posts a notification that the user taps to open the LSPosed Manager. This leaves no Magisk/KernelSU root artifacts and is much harder to detect via traditional root scans.

No injected lspd/lsposed libraries were found in system_server\'s process maps, no parasite config directory (/data/system/lspd/) exists, no staging files were found at /data/local/tmp/lspd, and no lspd notification channel was detected on the android package.
Disable LSPosed Parasite mode via the LSPosed Manager notification, then reboot. The lspd staging file at /data/local/tmp/lspd can be removed to prevent re-injection after reboot.
